one_line_takeaway: A controlled evaluation showing RAG poisoning robustness hinges on retriever choice (DPR > Contriever-MS) and on skeptical prompting that only helps models with strong enough reasoning -- while dilution fails and even the best defense stays below the no-retrieval baseline.
key_finding: Skeptical prompting improves adversarial robustness but only for strong models (GPT-4, GPT-4o, Claude-3.5, LLaMA-70b) and can HURT weaker ones (GPT-3.5, LLaMA-8b), so self-defense tracks reasoning capacity. Retriever choice matters greatly: DPR-based retrievers embed incorrect/adversarial passages farther from queries and are more robust, while Contriever-MS is the most vulnerable (most likely to surface adversarial content). Dilution by increasing k barely helps -- the absolute number of adversarial passages, not their proportion, drives degradation, because their high query-relevance lets them dominate. Guiding passages reliably counteract adversarial ones, especially under heavy pollution. Even with skeptical prompting, poisoned RAG still underperforms the Non-RAG baseline, an unresolved gap.
core_contribution: An empirical robustness study (not a new attack or defense system) of how RAG systems behave under adversarial corpus-poisoning. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a fine-grained taxonomy of retrieved context types -- Adversarial (crafted to mislead), Untouched (top clean corpus passage of lower relevance than the adversarial one), and Guiding (LLM-generated to steer toward the correct answer) -- used to run controlled experiments isolating each type's individual and combined effect on generation; and (2) a systematic analysis showing that 'skeptical prompting' can activate an LLM's internal reasoning to partially self-defend against poisoned passages, with effectiveness scaling with the model's reasoning/knowledge capacity. Note: the poisoning attack itself is NOT their contribution -- they reuse the black-box PoisonedRAG attack of Zou et al. (2024); the paper's value is diagnostic evaluation, not a new exploit or a new defense module.
threat_model: Corpus-poisoning against RAG: an attacker without access to the retriever architecture or target LLM injects a small number of adversarial passages (5 per target query) into the retrieval knowledge base. Each passage is generated by a black-box LLM (the PoisonedRAG method) from a target query-incorrect-answer pair so it has high query relevance and is likely retrieved into the top-k, thereby steering the LLM to emit a factually incorrect target answer. Injection rate is tiny (example: 0.0002% of the NQ corpus). The paper evaluates this threat rather than proposing it, then studies retriever choice and prompting as mitigations.
limitations: Restricted to three common QA datasets (NQ, HotpotQA, MS-MARCO), so generalization to other domains/tasks is untested. Studies essentially one attack family (PoisonedRAG global corpus poisoning); does not benchmark retriever-tailored or hybrid attacks. Even the best mitigation does not restore performance to the Non-RAG baseline, leaving a robustness gap. Uses the Non-RAG setting as a rough proxy for a model's internal knowledge, which is imprecise. Authors also flag a dual-use risk: the detailed injection recipes could aid attackers.

datasets_used: Natural Questions (NQ), Wikipedia KB with 2,681,468 passages
MS-MARCO, Bing web-document KB with 8,841,823 passages
quantitative_results: Adversarial passages reach top-k readily: on HotpotQA (Table 1), for every retriever at least 83% of queries have >=1 adversarial passage as top-1 (k=1); at k=10, >97% of HotpotQA queries and >83% of NQ queries retrieve an adversarial passage.

NQ k=1 adversarial retrieval rate (Table 1): Contriever 59, Contriever-MS 88, DPR-Single 54, DPR-Multi 47 (most robust), ANCE 66; guiding passages are retrieved MORE often than adversarial (e.g. DPR-Single NQ k=1 guiding 75 vs adv 54, Diff +21).
Combined NQ top-10 F1 (Table 2): skeptical prompting > neutral for strong models, e.g. Claude on Contriever 85.43 (skeptical) vs 75.94 (neutral); GPT-4 70.00 vs 44.90; GPT-4o 83.00 vs 67.69.
Best combined NQ F1 tends to come from DPR-Single or ANCE with skeptical prompting (e.g. Claude ANCE skeptical 89.45; GPT-4o DPR-Single skeptical 86.00; LLaMA-8b DPR-Single skeptical 45.23).

Table 2 highest-minus-lowest F1 spread per model (retriever+prompt choice impact): Claude 13.51, GPT-4 33.10, GPT-4o 22.73, GPT-3.5 17.18, LLaMA-8b 20.50, LLaMA-70b 15.30 -- i.e. >10% swing purely from retriever and prompt choice.
Pollution rate k=10 fixed, adversarial 1->5 (10%-50%): F1 declines monotonically for all models; skeptical prompting mitigates for GPT-4/GPT-4o/Claude but mildly for GPT-3.5.
Dilution: fixing 1 adversarial passage and raising k leaves F1 roughly flat, unlike the sharper gains from lowering pollution rate -- absolute adversarial count dominates.

Example top-10 for an NQ query (Table 4): 4 of the top-10 retrieved passages are injected adversarial (ranks 1,2,7,8), with the top adversarial passage scoring 1.406 vs 1.216 for the best untouched passage, at an injection rate of 0.0002%.
baselines_compared: Non-RAG (LLM answers from internal knowledge only) as the internal-knowledge reference point
Neutral prompting vs Skeptical prompting vs Faithful prompting (appendix)
Retrievers: Contriever, Contriever-MS, DPR-Single, DPR-Multi, ANCE
LLMs: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o, Claude-3.5, LLaMA3-8b, LLaMA3-70b
Context types: Adversarial vs Untouched vs Guiding (paper's own taxonomy)
